check: robots.txt exists and doesn't block important content
description: robots.txt is present at the domain root and its Disallow rules are reviewed to confirm they don't accidentally block pages meant to be indexed
why_it_matters: A single overly broad Disallow rule (e.g. blocking /app/ when marketing pages live under it) can deindex an entire site section without any other symptom

check: XML sitemap exists, is submitted to Search Console, and stays current
description: A sitemap.xml lists canonical URLs meant to be indexed, is referenced in robots.txt, submitted via Search Console, and regenerated automatically as pages are added/removed
why_it_matters: A stale or missing sitemap slows discovery of new pages and can leave removed pages appearing findable long after they're gone

check: No orphan pages — every indexable page reachable via internal links
description: Pages meant to be indexed are reachable through the site's own internal link structure, not only via the sitemap
why_it_matters: Search engines weight internal-link-discovered pages differently than sitemap-only pages, and orphan pages get proportionally less crawl attention and less inherited authority

check: Crawl budget not wasted on low-value URL patterns (faceted navigation, session IDs, infinite filters)
description: Faceted/filtered navigation and any parameter-driven URL variants are controlled via canonical tags, robots rules, or noindex so crawlers don't spend budget on near-duplicate variants
why_it_matters: On large sites, crawl budget genuinely constrains how often important pages get recrawled — wasting it on thousands of filter-combination URLs starves the pages that matter

check: No unintentional noindex tags on pages meant to rank
description: Every page meant to be indexed is checked for an accidental noindex meta tag or X-Robots-Tag header, especially after a staging-to-production deploy
why_it_matters: A noindex left over from a staging environment or a CMS default is one of the most common, highest-severity real-world SEO incidents — silent and easy to miss

check: Canonical tags point to the correct self-referencing or intended URL
description: Every indexable page has a canonical tag, either self-referencing or pointing deliberately at the preferred version among near-duplicates (with/without trailing slash, http/https, www/non-www)
why_it_matters: Missing or incorrect canonicals cause duplicate-content dilution — search engines may index the wrong URL variant or split ranking signals across near-duplicates

check: URL parameters handled consistently (canonical, or parameter handling in Search Console)
description: Tracking parameters, sort/filter parameters, and session identifiers either canonicalize to the clean URL or are explicitly configured in Search Console's parameter handling
why_it_matters: Unmanaged parameters multiply the number of near-duplicate URLs search engines encounter, diluting crawl budget and ranking signals across variants

check: 301 (permanent) redirects used for moved content, not 302
description: Content that has permanently moved uses a 301 status code; 302 is reserved for genuinely temporary redirects
why_it_matters: A 302 used for a permanent move doesn't reliably pass ranking signal the way a 301 does — this is a common mistake that quietly suppresses ranking transfer after a URL change

check: No redirect chains longer than one hop
description: A redirects directly to its final destination, not through two or three intermediate redirects
why_it_matters: Each additional hop adds latency (hurting Core Web Vitals) and some link-equity loss is commonly observed through long redirect chains

check: No redirect loops
description: No URL redirects to itself or forms a cycle with another URL
why_it_matters: A redirect loop makes the page completely inaccessible to both users and crawlers — an unambiguous critical bug when found

check: Entire site served over HTTPS with no mixed content
description: Every page and every resource on the page (images, scripts, styles) loads over HTTPS, with HTTP automatically redirecting to HTTPS
why_it_matters: HTTPS is a confirmed ranking signal and mixed content triggers browser security warnings that damage user trust — this should be a completely solved problem on any current site

check: HSTS header set to enforce HTTPS on repeat visits
description: Strict-Transport-Security header is present (this is SEC018's security-headers baseline applied specifically to its SEO-relevant benefit)
why_it_matters: Beyond the security benefit (SEC008), HSTS eliminates the HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect hop on repeat visits, a small but real performance/UX improvement

check: Mobile-first indexing verified — mobile version has full content parity with desktop
description: Google indexes primarily using the mobile version of a page — confirm the mobile rendering includes all content, structured data, and links present on desktop, not a stripped-down subset
why_it_matters: A mobile page missing content present on desktop effectively removes that content from what search engines actually index, even if desktop looks complete

check: Viewport meta tag configured correctly, no horizontal scroll on mobile
description: width=device-width, initial-scale=1 is set and no page content causes horizontal scrolling on mobile viewports
why_it_matters: This is table stakes for both mobile usability signals and basic user experience — a horizontal-scroll bug is highly visible and easy to catch, so its presence usually signals a broader QA gap

check: Critical content and links are present in the initial server response, not only client-rendered
description: Primary content and internal links are visible in the raw HTML response (via SSR/SSG) rather than requiring JavaScript execution to appear
why_it_matters: While major search engines do render JavaScript, rendering is a separate, delayed, resource-constrained pass — critical content client-rendered-only risks slower indexing and is a real risk for other crawlers (AI crawlers, some social previews) that render less reliably

check: No content hidden behind a required user interaction for indexing purposes
description: Content meant to be indexed (not deliberately gated content) doesn't require a click, hover, or scroll-triggered lazy-load beyond what search engine renderers reliably execute
why_it_matters: Interaction-gated content is a common way meaningful page content becomes invisible to crawlers even when the site looks fine to a human visitor

check: Logical, shallow URL hierarchy — important pages reachable within 3 clicks from the homepage
description: Site architecture keeps important pages a small number of clicks from the homepage, reflected in both navigation and URL structure
why_it_matters: Pages buried deep in the site architecture receive less crawl frequency and less inherited internal-link authority than pages closer to the homepage

check: Breadcrumb navigation present and marked up with BreadcrumbList schema
description: Pages beyond the top level show breadcrumb navigation reflecting their place in the site hierarchy, with matching structured data
why_it_matters: Breadcrumbs help both users and crawlers understand site structure, and BreadcrumbList schema can produce a breadcrumb trail directly in search results

check: hreflang implemented correctly for any multi-language/multi-region content
description: Pages targeting different languages/regions use hreflang tags with correct language-region codes and reciprocal linking between all variants including a self-reference
why_it_matters: Incorrect hreflang (missing reciprocal links, wrong codes, missing self-reference) is one of the most common international SEO implementation errors and can cause the wrong regional page to rank in the wrong market

check: Language/region detection doesn't force-redirect based on IP/browser locale alone
description: Users and crawlers can access any language/region version directly by URL, without being forcibly redirected based solely on detected location
why_it_matters: Forced geo/locale redirects can prevent crawlers from accessing and indexing non-default regional versions at all, and frustrate users wanting a specific version deliberately

check: Server response time (TTFB) is fast, not just client-side rendering
description: Time to First Byte is measured and optimized independently of client-side rendering performance — a fast frontend can't compensate for a slow server response
why_it_matters: TTFB is foundational to every downstream performance metric (LCP can't beat TTFB) and is also a factor search engines weigh directly, separate from the Core Web Vitals covered in `QA040`-`QA042`

check: Render-blocking resources minimized on the critical path (see QA048)
description: This is `QA048` restated with its SEO-specific framing — render-blocking CSS/JS on the critical path directly delays both LCP and search engines' ability to assess above-the-fold content quickly
why_it_matters: Slow-rendering pages both rank worse (Core Web Vitals is a ranking signal) and get less thorough crawling within a given crawl budget window

check: Structured data validates without errors (Rich Results Test / Schema Validator)
description: Every schema.org markup block on the site passes validation with no errors, checked after any template or CMS change that could affect it
why_it_matters: Invalid structured data doesn't just fail to earn rich results — in some cases a sufficiently broken implementation can trigger a manual action for structured data spam if it materially misrepresents the page

check: Structured data accurately reflects visible page content
description: Every fact asserted in JSON-LD (price, rating, availability, author) matches what's actually visible on the rendered page — see `EC024`'s inventory-sync-specific version of this
why_it_matters: Structured data that doesn't match visible content violates Google's structured data guidelines and risks losing rich-result eligibility or a manual action

check: Crawler behavior monitored via server logs, not assumed from Search Console alone
description: For larger/established sites, raw server logs are periodically analyzed to see which pages search engine crawlers actually visit and how often, not inferred solely from Search Console's sampled reporting
why_it_matters: Search Console data is sampled and delayed — log files show ground-truth crawler behavior, revealing crawl budget waste or unexpected blocking that dashboard tools can miss entirely

check: Custom 404 page exists and returns a genuine 404 status code
description: Broken URLs show a helpful custom error page while the underlying HTTP status code is genuinely 404 (not 200 with 404-looking content — a 'soft 404')
why_it_matters: A soft 404 (returning 200 for a not-found page) confuses crawlers into treating broken pages as valid content, polluting the index with error pages

check: Broken internal links found and fixed proactively
description: Internal links pointing to 404s or redirect chains are found via a periodic crawl and fixed, not left to accumulate indefinitely
why_it_matters: Broken internal links waste crawl budget, dilute internal link equity, and directly hurt user experience for anyone who clicks them

check: No substantial duplicate content across pages without canonicalization
description: Pages with substantially similar content (print versions, tracking-parameter variants, staging content indexed accidentally) are consolidated via canonical tags or 301s, not left as separate indexable pages
why_it_matters: Duplicate content dilutes ranking signal across multiple URLs instead of consolidating it onto the one page that should actually rank

check: Migration/redesign includes a full URL redirect map, not just a domain swap
description: Any domain change, URL structure change, or platform migration maps every old URL to its new equivalent with 301 redirects before launch, not left to 404 or handled reactively afterward
why_it_matters: Un-redirected migrations are one of the most common causes of a sudden, severe organic traffic drop — this is entirely preventable with proper pre-launch planning

check: Search Console change-of-address / new property set up and monitored post-migration
description: After a domain or major structural migration, Search Console is updated (change of address tool where applicable) and crawl/index status is monitored closely for the following weeks
why_it_matters: Migration-related ranking drops are far easier to diagnose and recover from when caught within days via active monitoring rather than discovered weeks later via a traffic report

check: AI crawlers (GPTBot, PerplexityBot, ClaudeBot, etc.) are deliberately allowed or blocked, not left to CMS defaults
description: robots.txt explicitly makes a deliberate decision about each major AI crawler rather than inheriting whatever a CMS or hosting platform defaulted to
why_it_matters: An unreviewed default can either accidentally block AI crawlers from a site wanting AI-search visibility, or accidentally allow crawlers a site owner would have preferred to restrict — this is a decision worth making explicitly, not by default

check: llms.txt present for sites prioritizing AI-search/LLM citability
description: A llms.txt file at the domain root provides a curated, LLM-friendly summary of the site's key content and structure, following the emerging llms.txt convention
why_it_matters: For sites where AI Overviews/ChatGPT/Perplexity citation matters as a traffic source, llms.txt is a low-cost, emerging signal that helps LLM-based crawlers understand site structure faster than inferring it from a full crawl

check: Core Web Vitals monitored via field data (CrUX), not lab scores alone
description: This is `QA050` applied to SEO specifically — Google's ranking systems use field data (Chrome UX Report), not Lighthouse lab scores, so field data is what should be monitored for SEO purposes
why_it_matters: A page can look great in a local Lighthouse run and still be a Core Web Vitals ranking liability if real users on real devices/networks experience it differently

check: Publish and last-modified dates are accurate and exposed via structured data
description: Article/BlogPosting schema's datePublished and dateModified fields reflect genuine publish/update dates, not artificially bumped dates with no real content change
why_it_matters: Search engines have become more resistant to freshness-gaming (bumping a date with no real update) — accurate dates build trust signal over time, while detected gaming can work against a site's credibility
